videos.title: Fable Is Back: Claude Code, AI Agents & Fitness Tech Livestream
videos.description:
An unedited livestream on where AI agents feel right now: Fable coming back, why Claude Code still feels like the strongest harness for my workflow, how Cursor comparisons can get messy, and why the model itself is only part of the story.
The big thread here is that LLMs do not really “edit videos” or “pull fitness data” by themselves. The harness, tools, APIs, local scripts, devices, and workflows around the model are where a huge amount of the intelligence actually shows up.
I also get into open/local models, Hermes agents, Google Antigravity, Project Aria, Agent Stack Daily, training dashboards, heart meds, running, lifting, and why jiu-jitsu is still worth doing even when it wrecks you.
Not medical, legal, financial, or training advice. Just personal experience, AI commentary, and live thinking out loud.

videos.title: AEKE K1 vs Speediance: The Camera Changes Everything
videos.description:
This video is really about whether the AEKE IKE K1 solves the biggest smart gym problems better than the competition. Joel has owned it for 6 months, and this is the kind of real-world demo buyers actually need before spending the money.

Joel bought the AEKE IKE K1 after comparing it to other smart home gyms like Speediance and Tonal, and in this interview he breaks down what it's actually like to live with. The K1 is a standalone smart gym with no wall mount required, it folds up into a mirror when not in use, and it's clearly aimed at people who want premium home fitness tech without committing to a subscription.

The biggest story here is the camera. AEKE built the skeletal tracking camera directly into the bottom of the machine, facing up, so it can track your movement and form in real time with instant response. That's a very different experience from systems that rely on your phone camera, where feedback can feel slower, less consistent, and less integrated. If you care about form coaching, this is the feature that makes the K1 stand out.

On the software side, AEKE is doing a lot right. The AI coach can build workouts based on your goals, available time, and equipment, then adapt from your strength assessment. You can also build your own workouts by choosing exercises, sets, and reps in the app and sending them to the machine. Beyond strength training, the K1 also supports yoga, qigong, boxing, and Pilates with camera tracking, and Joel talks about the quarterly software updates, user-voted roadmap, and the fact that these features are included with no subscription.

Hardware-wise, the K1 has a sharp 4K screen, a clean fold-up design, and wheels on the back so it can be moved more easily than a wall-mounted system. Joel also covers the quirks, including moving it on tile, the current resistance limits, and how some users are adding more weight with cable and pulley hacks. After 6 months, his take is that the K1 was a smart buy, especially given AEKE's support and update pace. He also shares thoughts on the newly announced AEKE K2 and why K1 owners shouldn't panic.

videos.title: Speediance at 1.2M Lbs Lifted: Why I Still Recommend It Over Tonal
videos.description:
0:00 V3.1 Software — Final Verdict
0:39 Progressive Overload Bug on Unilateral Movements
2:03 Lifting vs Running (35,000 lbs Yesterday)
2:23 SPEEDIANCE Challenges & Physical Medals
3:47 Do I Still Love the Original SPEEDIANCE?
6:09 Retraction Issue — Reporting to the Dev Team
7:30 Open Source Desktop App for SPEEDIANCE Data
8:51 Building My Own AI Training Dashboard
10:26 Huge V3.1 Feature I Almost Forgot (Pop-Ups Are Gone!)
11:55 Bulk Season — 230 lbs & Targeting Strength
12:13 SPEEDIANCE vs Fit Transformer, Tonal & Voltras
13:40 Lifetime Pounds Lifted: 1.2 Million & Climbing
14:20 Smart Handle Disconnect Issue
15:33 Wrap Up

1.2 million pounds lifted on Speediance — and I'm still choosing it over Tonal. Here's why the V3.1 software upgrade matters, what bugs remain, and why Speediance keeps winning for independent lifters who refuse forced memberships.

Progressive overload finally works (mostly). The V3.1 update added pop-up prompts asking if you want to increase weight after sets — complete with options to choose between increments or skip entirely. I dig into where it works (bilateral and bar movements) versus where it still breaks (unilateral movements have a bug where they don't trigger prompts properly). The workaround? Manually clicking the four-dot menu and selecting "move playlist" before working sets. Annoying, but functional.

I'm currently at 230 lbs in full bulk season, prioritizing strength over cardio. That said, lifting 35,000 pounds yesterday definitely impacted my run today — which is why I'm selecting "none" on overload prompts for now. I also explore the new Challenges feature (complete with physical medals they mail to gold finishers) and explain why hitting gold beats settling for silver every time.

One thing Speediance nailed that Tonal never would: they removed the membership pop-ups from the main screen. Tonal would never do that — they moved action buttons to reserve screen real estate for ads. Speediance listened to community feedback. That matters.

I'm also building something cool: an AI-powered training dashboard using an open-source desktop app that reads Speediance data. It correlates my lifting with running, BJJ, Garmin, and Whoop data for a complete training picture. I'll share the link in the description — it's a game-changer for anyone who wants full data ownership.

A few issues: retraction speed when moving rails (reporting to the dev team), and my right smart handle disconnecting faster than the left. Neither is deal-breaking. No ring problems, no downtime after 1.2 million pounds lifted.
Still comparing Fit Transformer and Voltra? Fit Transformer isn't a smart home gym — no progressive overload, no programming. Voltra has a great idea Speediance will is about to copy. For my money, Speediance wins because of progressive overload.

videos.title: Speediance 2S V3.1 Review: New Safety Start, Firmware Changes & Real‑World Lifting
videos.description:
Speediance V3.1 is here on my upstairs 2S, and after a few days of real workouts with the new firmware, I’m convinced “Safety Start” is not the safety feature it’s advertised to be.  In this video I walk through the good, the bad, and the ugly of the V3.1 software and firmware combo, including slower cable retraction, unsafe slack behavior, and a massive downgrade to partner and dual‑load modes compared to the old software.
We’ll test Safety Start in free lift, barbell, and handle movements, and I’ll show why it was clearly built to support upcoming Pilates features rather than to actually fix the real safety problems lifters were seeing.  I also demo how the new firmware makes retraction noticeably worse than on my original Speediance downstairs, why dual‑load mode is more broken than ever, and how bringing back partner lifting without independent sides or a true guest profile is a step backward, not an upgrade.
If you own a Speediance 2S or original Gym Monster and you’re wondering whether to update, how to stay safe with heavy eccentric work, and whether Safety Start should be part of your normal setup, this breakdown is for you.  I’ll also cover how I actually lift (warm‑ups, one‑second pause vs relying on software “safety,” and why I’d rather risk the machine than my spine) so you can decide what makes sense in your own home gym.
Timestamps (edit to taste):
 • 00:00 Intro – Speediance 2S vs original and V3.1 context
 • 03:10 New firmware, retraction changes, and why it feels worse
 • 08:25 Safety Start demo in free lift (and why I almost never use it)
 • 15:40 Dual‑load & partner mode: from real safety feature to downgrade
 • 24:10 Guest profiles, missing second user support, and UI gaps
 • 32:00 When Safety Start “helps” vs when it hurts your range of motion
 • 40:15 My real‑world lifting workflow and safety priorities

comments.author: @BeatriceJollie
comments.text: Hi Toby, just ordered a GM2S, 1-2 weeks delivery, so it might be a bit late to ask now, but I have discovered that sometimes, especially with heavier weight, the barbell can feel uneven, unbalanced, causing wobbling.  As you have a GM1 and a GM2S, could you please comment on this as it is a bit concerning.

replies.author: @tobyonfitness
replies.text: That is a hand placement, body placement, or bench placement thing. I've noticed especially with their bench if I'm not dead center it will be uneven a bit. If you tilt too far it turns the weight off but it has to be very far. Tonal had this same issue but it's exaggerated by the speediance having multiple bar attachment positions so it takes even more time to get the positioning right.

comments.author: @BeatriceJollie
comments.text:
I’ve been waiting for Gym Monster 3 as the GM2 is surely due an update this year 2006?
I’m imagining the GM3 to have some really desirable features such as extendable arms or taller frame or more durable motors or a Bluetooth speaker I can use with Spotify, etc., that I would hate to miss out on, but Speediance have now launched the GM2S in the UK. I do have a home gym but I could do so much more on the Gym Monster. I noticed that you still rate your GM1 even though some say it’s not as smooth as the GM2 (you don’t have a GM2 I think), although I think I read on your site somewhere that you compared the GM2S to feeling more like a lawnmower compared to the GM1? Anyway, with your finger more on the Speediance pulse than most, do you think I should wait for the GM3 or bite the bullet on the GM2S. Also, whilst I’m here and hopeful of a reply, I understand that both the GM2 and the GM2S have have exactly the same motors, so presumably the clever firmware/software on the GM2S is asking more of the same hardware and so will wear out sooner? Many thanks, Beatrice :)

replies.author: @tobyonfitness
replies.text: No, they can’t back port the changes from the 2s to the 2. The motors are the same and in fact are the same as the 1 but realize the 1 is capable of 500+lbs; The cables and the frame are not. The 2s has this super capacitor to return power to the unit. Spotify I believe is now available on the 2/2s through connecting to your phone and you can hack that solution onto the 1 (by going manually to the Bluetooth Android settings)… Unfortunately if the 3 were coming this year I think they would have demo’ed it at CES (like they did every other product they are making). I like that old lawnmower pull feel that I believe is exclusive to the 2s but I could see how some wouldn’t. Yes people online say the 2 has the smoothest pull and that is because of the slight alterations to the cabling system to handle an extra 60lbs. We only got the 2s because the 2 was a joke compared to the Tonal 2, my gut reaction is they will not make the mistake of going first again and that is why we are getting the mini (voltra clone) and their whoop clone this year… I could be totally wrong but I don’t think we are getting a 3 based on it not being at CES. I have a harder time recommending the 1 especially if you want to do Pilates because that’s not coming although I still love that device and am glad I bought it. I personally think these machines are 1000x safer than a home gym it’s just what does your budget look like for which I would choose. The motors are crazy durable (I’ve never heard of anyone frying one) it’s the cables and the connectors (and the accessories) that people have massive issues with.

comments.author: @scottygadlin7218
comments.text: I found the safety start to be helpful for tricep rope movements like cable crunches.  It allows me to not have to wear the ring.  Otherwise I agree it is not very useful. videos.title: Build Your Best Training Split: Arnold Blueprint (PPL), Full Body, or Bro Split?
videos.description:
Welcome back to Toby On Fitness Tech! In this video, I finally answer a question I’ve been getting since my extreme six‑month transformation: “What training split do you use?” Instead of a simple “push–pull–legs” or “bro split” answer, I walk through why the real answer is: it depends on your goals, your recovery, and your schedule today.

I break down the exact Arnold Blueprint–style push–pull–legs program I used on Tonal in 2023 to go from morbidly obese to fit under a massive calorie deficit, including how I modified it into blocks to make it work on a smart cable machine. Then I contrast that with the full‑body (minus legs) Speediance programs I built afterward, how I structure warm‑up sets vs. hard sets, and why I stopped trying to lift every single day.

You’ll also hear how a daily “lunar challenge” forced me into a classic bro split, what actually grew best on that approach (hello, arms day), and how jiu-jitsu, running, and injury history shape my current split and priorities. The goal of this video is to help you pick the split that you’ll actually stick to—whether that’s PPL, full body, or a body-part split—so you can build muscle safely and sustainably with the time and tools you have.

If you’re into smart home gyms like Speediance or Tonal, cross‑training with jiu‑jitsu, or just trying to figure out the best way to structure your week without burning out, this one’s for you. I went from tonal w/ subscription to tonal plus a complicated spreadsheet that did everything that these PR calculations do and even that was enough of a barrier to entry that I lifted 50% less than usual when I cancelled the tonal subscription before buying the speediance. The thing I would worry about more is these are tech devices and I estimate have a shelf life of 5 to 10 years max where a functional trainer will last forever. For me I would rather have something I use everyday and lasts 5 years than something that sits in my garage collecting dust (I actually had a functional trainer that I donated to my BJJ gym because that was the case with it) but that is just me and I am sure others enjoy keeping a logbook of their workouts. I had a personal trainer in the past when I was doing BJJ competitions and they kept track of all that for me and when Covid shut them down I was stuck doing it myself and I both hurt myself doing the wrong exercises with to much progression and really disliked tracking it all myself with a spreadsheet.

How dynamic weight modes (Standard, Chain, Eccentric) function

How your custom workouts and program workouts share data
